title: Designing Tool Interfaces for LLM Agents

blocks.html:
A "tool" is a function you let Claude call — search the catalog, send an email, issue a refund. Claude chooses tools by reading their names, descriptions, and parameter definitions, the way you'd choose an appliance by reading its label and manual. So tool design is really communication design: a good interface makes the right action easy and the wrong action hard or impossible.

The core instinct to build: don't tell the model rules the schema could enforce. If a cardio workout needs duration and a strength workout needs sets and reps, one do-everything tool with optional fields invites nonsense combinations. Two separate tools make the invalid combinations literally unexpressible.

Where a tool runs matters too. Client-side tools run in your app (full control, full responsibility). Server-side tools run on Anthropic's infrastructure (no ops burden, but you can't intercept calls). MCP tools run wherever that server lives. Promote an action to a dedicated client-side tool when you need to gate, audit, or confirm it — a send_email call can be intercepted; a shell command doing the same thing is an opaque string.

A user says "delete the Henderson project." There are two projects with Henderson in the name. If your delete tool accepts a name string, the model guesses — and one day guesses wrong. The safe pattern is lookup-then-act: a search tool returns candidate IDs with distinguishing details (owner, last activity), the user picks the right one with a click, and the delete tool only accepts IDs. For truly destructive actions, add preview-then-execute: a preview tool returns the impact plus a one-time confirmation token, and the execute tool requires that token. Note what this replaces: a dry_run: true flag is worthless because the model itself sets it.

Output design gets tested as much as input design:

  • Return structured results with IDs downstream tools can use — never just prose.
  • "No matches" is a successful empty result, not an error; conflating them makes agents retry valid queries.
  • Paginate: first page + total_count + cursor. Don't auto-fetch hundreds of items.
  • Normalize heterogeneous backend data into one consistent schema before the model sees it.
  • For ML outputs with confidence scores, return a calibrated requires_review boolean with reasons — raw scores invite over-trust and over-escalation.

For big tool catalogs, use progressive availability: discovery tools return a shortlist, then the chosen tools are dynamically added — never a single find_and_execute that hides the final decision. And compose tools only when no judgment lives between the steps; "check availability + book" should be atomic (race condition), but "retrieve sources + write conclusions" must stay separate.

items: Schemas enforce; descriptions advise. Make invalid calls unrepresentable.
Lookup-then-act: search returns IDs, mutations accept only IDs, users disambiguate.
dry_run flags are model-controlled and useless; preview + one-time token is structural.
Empty result ≠ error. Success-with-nothing and failure must be distinguishable.
Compose tools for mechanical/atomic sequences; never to hide decisions.
